patches.rationale: The brief explicitly defines the product as a governed fronting layer over Superset with a bounded capability surface and no raw tool exposure.
patches.value: Provide a governed ANIP fronting service for Apache Superset so agents can discover allowed analytics context, answer bounded analytics questions, create chart previews, and prepare governed publication or dataset draft requests without direct access to raw Superset tools.

patches.rationale: The source frames the core problem as needing governed analytics behavior instead of exposing raw SQL, chart, dashboard, dataset, or database operations directly to agents.
patches.value: Native Superset APIs and MCP-style tool exposure can give agents access to low-level analytics operations, but the organization needs business-level governance over what analytics actions are visible, how questions are answered, when previews are allowed, and where approval, denial, and audit boundaries must stop higher-risk actions.

patches.rationale: These goals are directly supported by the purpose, policy semantics, and governed capability surface in the brief.
value: Expose organization-approved analytics capabilities instead of raw Superset or MCP tool operations.
Let agents answer analytics questions only within actor-visible, allowlisted dataset and metric boundaries.
Default exploratory analytics work to read-only or preview behavior.
Require explicit approval for chart publication, dashboard publication, and dataset creation workflows.
Deny raw SQL, raw exports, protected database access, and other bypass paths that would weaken governance.
Produce audit-friendly outcomes with clear clarification, denial, preview, and approval states.

patches.rationale: The governed capability surface provides stable user task families that the product should answer or support.
This summarizes the policy semantics and fronting intent in PM-readable terms while preserving the core governance controls.
patches.value: The product only exposes governed analytics capabilities backed by native Superset REST APIs. It limits work to actor-visible and allowlisted datasets, metrics, dimensions, and scopes; defaults chart creation and similar exploratory work to preview-only or read-only behavior; asks for clarification when required context is missing; denies raw SQL, raw exports, protected database access, and publication bypass; and keeps outcomes audit-friendly.

patches.rationale: The source clearly distinguishes direct low-risk actions from approval-gated publishing and dataset creation workflows, making this field lockable.
patches.value: Discovery, bounded question answering, and chart preview can proceed within governed limits, but saving or updating charts, publishing dashboards, and creating datasets are approval-gated and must stop for analytics owner or BI admin review before execution.

patches.rationale: The brief provides concrete, reviewable product intent for the required summary fields and explicit governance boundaries, so the product summary can be treated as ready to lock once accepted.

questions_for_user: Should the product summary explicitly state the primary user groups, such as internal agents, analytics owners, and BI admins?
Do you want the business goals to emphasize speed of analytics access, governance compliance, or both as co-equal outcomes?
Should dashboard draft preparation and dataset draft preparation be framed as core user-facing outcomes or as secondary governed workflows?
envelope.summary: Draft PM-facing product summary patch candidates based on the Superset governed fronting brief. The brief is strong enough to propose lockable purpose, problem, goals, supported question families, governed behavior, and approval posture centered on governed analytics access over native Superset APIs without exposing raw tools.
envelope.title: Business Summary Proposal: Superset Fronting Showcase 0.2.0
watchouts: The brief supports a governed analytics fronting service, not a generic analytics assistant; avoid broadening scope beyond allowed datasets, metrics, dimensions, previews, and approval-gated publishing or dataset drafting.
Do not describe raw SQL, raw exports, direct database access, or MCP tool exposure as supported behavior; the source explicitly denies or excludes them.
Approval boundaries are asymmetric: discovery, bounded question answering, and chart preview can proceed directly, while chart publish, dashboard publish, and dataset creation stop for approval.
The source says real integration evidence must be added before contract generation; product summary language should not imply backend operations have already been validated from live evidence.

next_steps: Confirm the minimum set of business actors you want represented in the actor model.

After role confirmation, tighten each actor's visibility, action, and approval expectations against the governed capability surface.

patches.rationale: The brief clearly describes a user who consumes governed analytics without raw tool access.

value.action_expectations: Can discover allowed analytics context and ask bounded analytics questions. May receive preview-style results only. Must not submit raw SQL, request unrestricted exports, bypass dataset allowlists, or switch to protected databases.
value.approval_expectations: Does not approve governed changes. Read-only and preview interactions should not require this actor to obtain approval unless the request shifts into publish, save, or dataset-creation flows.

value.notes: This actor is implied by the preview-create, chart-publish-request, and dashboard-draft-prepare capabilities. If you prefer, this can be split into separate chart creator and dashboard creator actors.

value.title: Analytics Creator

value.summary: Named approver role for governed analytics publication decisions.

patches.rationale: The source explicitly names BI admin as a chart publication approver and should be modeled separately unless PM chooses to merge approvers.

patches.rationale: The brief defines a separate dataset-draft workflow with approval gating and no raw SQL, implying a distinct requester role or at least a distinct actor posture.

value.action_expectations: Can prepare dataset drafts by stating dataset purpose, query intent, and optional source tables or metrics. Cannot provide raw SQL, create unapproved saved queries, or bypass protected database restrictions.
value.approval_expectations: Dataset draft creation is approval-gated, but the approving actor is not named in the source. This actor should be treated as a requester, not an approver.

envelope.summary: Patch candidates translate the brief into PM-facing permission posture for governed Superset analytics. envelope.title: Permission Intent Patch Proposal: Superset governed analytics fronting
watchouts: The canonical vocabulary lists are empty, so these patches rely on the accepted in-flight actor and business-area IDs embedded in the source text.
The brief defines business trust posture, not implementation RBAC or API-level authorization mechanics.
Several restrictions apply across actors, especially raw SQL, raw exports, protected database access, and bypass of approval flows.
Some outcomes depend on missing required context such as dataset, metric, chart preview, dashboard scope, or publish reason; those should remain clarification stops rather than silent denials.

next_steps: Review and accept, edit, or reject the candidate requirement blocks below.
Add real integration evidence such as OpenAPI, API docs, or live discovery for the Superset native REST endpoints in scope.

items.body: The product shall present a governed analytics capability surface for discovering allowed analytics context, answering bounded analytics questions, creating chart previews, requesting chart publication, preparing dashboard drafts, and preparing dataset drafts. Agents shall not receive raw SQL, raw chart, raw dashboard, raw database, or MCP-style tool exposure.
items.rationale: The source repeatedly states that the fronting layer must own analytics semantics and must not expose raw Superset or MCP tools directly.
items.title: Expose only governed analytics capabilities to agents

items.body: The product shall integrate with Apache Superset through native REST APIs as the durable backend for governed analytics operations. The implementation shall treat MCP only as a comparison surface and not as the execution interface for the governed contract.
items.rationale: The brief explicitly names native Superset REST APIs as the backend integration and says MCP is intentionally not used as the ANIP execution interface.
items.title: Use native Superset REST APIs as the backend execution binding

items.body: The product shall support discovery of allowed datasets, charts, dashboards, metrics, and dimensions within an actor-visible workspace scope. Discovery results shall be limited to allowlisted and visible assets and returned as read-only catalog summaries.
items.rationale: The governed capability surface defines discovery behavior and the policy semantics require actor-visible and allowlisted scope.
items.title: Provide governed discovery of actor-visible analytics context

items.body: The product shall answer analytics questions only when the request is anchored to an allowed dataset and governed metric or dimension context. Execution shall remain bounded by declared dataset scope, metric and dimension constraints, result grain, row limits, and audit constraints. Raw SQL shall not be accepted as an input.
items.rationale: The source defines bounded analytics answers and explicitly prohibits raw SQL while requiring governance over metrics, dimensions, grain, and limits.
items.title: Answer analytics questions only within bounded dataset and metric constraints

items.body: The product shall default chart creation and dashboard preparation workflows to preview-only behavior. Preview flows shall not save or publish artifacts unless an explicit approval-gated path is completed.
items.rationale: The brief states preview-only defaults and says chart creation does not save by default, while dashboard work requires approval before publish.
items.title: Default creation workflows to preview-only outcomes

items.body: The product shall require explicit approval before saving or updating charts, publishing dashboards, or creating virtual or semantic dataset artifacts. The system shall capture a publish or creation reason and stop at an approval boundary until an authorized analytics owner or BI administrator approves the request.
items.rationale: Approval gating is a central policy in the source, including required reason input and named approver roles for publication actions.
items.title: Require approval for publication and dataset creation actions

items.body: The product shall return a clarification-required outcome when required context is missing, including missing dataset reference, metric, chart preview reference, dashboard scope, or publish reason. The clarification response shall identify the missing governed input needed to continue.
items.rationale: The policy semantics explicitly call out clarification-required behavior for missing dataset, metric, preview, dashboard scope, or publish reason.
items.title: Return clarification-required outcomes when required context is missing

items.body: The product shall enforce dataset, chart, dashboard, and database allowlists and may restrict owner-level, row-level, customer-level, or otherwise sensitive dimensions even when the downstream platform would technically allow access. Governance rules shall take precedence over raw backend reachability.
items.rationale: The source says actor-visible and allowlisted scope is mandatory and explicitly notes that sensitive dimensions may be restricted even if backend RBAC allows the query.
items.title: Enforce allowlists and sensitive-dimension restrictions beyond backend RBAC

items.body: The product shall allow backend options only for bounded provider controls such as selected fields, pagination, chart rendering format, or preview output format. Backend options shall not permit arbitrary SQL execution, unbounded export, database switching, allowlist bypass, or hidden publication behavior.
items.rationale: The source defines a narrow set of acceptable backend options and explicitly lists prohibited behaviors.
items.title: Limit backend options to bounded provider controls

items.body: The product shall deny requests for raw SQL, raw exports, unrestricted SQL execution, protected database access, dashboard publication bypass, and unapproved saved-query creation. Denial outcomes shall be explicit and audit-friendly.
items.rationale: The policy semantics enumerate these denied behaviors directly.
items.title: Deny prohibited raw access and bypass behaviors

items.body: The product shall produce audit-friendly outcomes for discovery, bounded answers, previews, approval requests, denials, and clarification stops. Records shall preserve enough context to explain what was requested, what governance boundary applied, and why the system returned the resulting posture.
items.rationale: Audit is repeatedly named in the brief, but the exact record schema is not yet defined.
items.title: Produce audit-friendly records for governed actions, denials, and approvals

items.body: Before contract generation, the product definition process shall include real integration evidence for the downstream Superset operations in scope, such as uploaded API documentation, schemas, permission matrices, live discovery, or manually entered operations. The process shall not infer backend operations from project name or domain alone.
items.rationale: The fronting intent includes an explicit next review step requiring real source evidence and warns against inventing backend operations.
items.title: Require real integration evidence before contract generation

questions_for_user: Which actor groups should be in scope for v1 approval flows: analytics owner only, BI admin only, or either?
Should bounded analytics answers be limited to preview/read-only responses only, or can some answers create reusable artifacts after approval?
What specific row limits, time-window limits, and result-grain restrictions should apply by default?
What audit fields are mandatory for every governed action and denial outcome?
What evidence source will be added next to validate the native Superset REST operations before contract generation?
envelope.summary: Candidate PM requirement blocks derived from the supplied brief for a governed ANIP fronting layer over Apache Superset native REST APIs. Focus is on bounded analytics capabilities, approval gates, denial rules, clarification behavior, and audit-friendly outcomes without exposing raw Superset tools.
envelope.title: Requirement Proposal: Governed Superset Fronting Service
watchouts: The brief is strong on governance intent but does not yet define concrete numeric limits for rows, pagination, or time windows.
Approval semantics are clear at a policy level, but approver routing, SLA, and approval record shape are not specified.
The source says native Superset REST APIs are the execution binding, but Studio should still verify exact supported operations from real integration evidence before contract generation.
Dataset, metric, dimension, and dashboard allowlists are required, but ownership and maintenance workflow for those allowlists are not described.
Clarification behavior is named for missing context, but the exact response structure and retry expectations are still open.

next_steps: Review and accept or reject each candidate scenario block individually.
Replace generic workspace and dataset references with real allowlisted examples from the intended rollout.
Confirm the approval actor model for chart publish and any future dashboard publish path.
Add backend integration evidence from OpenAPI, API docs, live discovery, or manual operation entry before moving to contract generation.

items.body: An analytics consumer asks what datasets, charts, dashboards, metrics, and dimensions are available in a workspace. The service returns only actor-visible, allowlisted analytics context and keeps the interaction read-only.

scenario.actor_context: Analytics consumer or creator with access to a governed workspace
scenario.business_scope: Governed analytics discovery over allowlisted Superset assets
expected_anip_support: Enforce workspace and asset visibility before any backend lookup.
Present a business-level discovery capability instead of direct dataset, chart, dashboard, or database tools.
Record audit-friendly discovery outcomes.
expected_behavior: Results are read-only and limited to actor-visible, allowlisted analytics assets.

scenario.name: discover-allowed-analytics-context-within-a-governed-workspace
scenario.narrative: A user starts with a business question but first needs to understand what governed analytics assets are available. They provide a workspace scope and search query, and the service returns only the datasets, charts, dashboards, metrics, and dimensions they are allowed to see.

orchestration_steps.action: User provides workspace_scope and a discovery query, optionally narrowing by asset type or limit.
Service checks actor visibility and dataset, chart, and dashboard allowlists for the requested workspace.
Service queries native Superset catalog endpoints for matching datasets, charts, and dashboards within governed bounds.
Service returns catalog summaries with only allowed metrics and dimensions exposed to the actor.

scenario.time_scope: Ad hoc at the start of an analytics task

items.title: Discover allowed analytics context within a governed workspace

items.body: An analytics consumer asks a business question against a specific allowed dataset. The service answers using governed metrics, dimensions, time windows, and filters, without accepting raw SQL or returning unrestricted exports.
items.rationale: The brief clearly defines bounded analytics answering, required dataset reference, optional governed inputs, preview/read-only posture, and denial of raw SQL.

scenario.actor_context: Analytics consumer, creator, or owner with bounded analytics access
scenario.business_scope: Read-only governed analytics answers over allowlisted datasets
expected_anip_support: Map business questions to bounded analytics execution without exposing raw Superset query tools.
Enforce metric, dimension, row-limit, and allowlist policy before backend execution.
Return explicit clarification or denial outcomes when policy or required inputs are not satisfied.
Capture audit-friendly records of the governed answer path.
expected_behavior: The service should require dataset_ref and stop for clarification if it is missing.
Only governed metrics, dimensions, filters, and result grain should be allowed.
Raw SQL must not be accepted as input or exposed as output.
Sensitive, owner-level, row-level, or customer-level dimensions may be restricted even if downstream RBAC would allow them.
scenario.name: answer-a-governed-analytics-question-against-an-allowed-dataset
scenario.narrative: A user asks a business question such as understanding performance over a time window, but must do so within an allowed dataset and governed metric and dimension boundaries. The service returns a bounded answer rather than exposing query construction or SQL execution.

orchestration_steps.action: User submits a question and dataset_ref, optionally including metric, dimension, time_window, filters, and limit.
Service validates that the dataset is actor-visible and allowlisted, and that requested metrics and dimensions are governed and permitted.
Service executes bounded semantic retrieval or chart-data access within declared row, grain, and policy constraints.
Service returns a governed answer or preview result with no raw SQL and no unrestricted export path.

items.title: Answer a governed analytics question against an allowed dataset

items.body: A user asks an analytics question or publish request without the required dataset, metric, preview reference, dashboard scope, or reason. The service does not guess; it asks for the minimum missing context before continuing.
items.rationale: The source explicitly states that missing dataset, metric, chart preview, dashboard scope, or publish reason returns clarification_required.

scenario.actor_context: Any otherwise authorized actor using governed analytics capabilities
scenario.business_scope: Input completeness and safe continuation rules across analytics workflows
expected_anip_support: Make required inputs explicit in the capability contract.
Return structured clarification outcomes instead of hidden fallback behavior.
Preserve auditability of why execution did not proceed.
expected_behavior: The service should not invent dataset, metric, preview, dashboard scope, or publish reason values.
Clarification should be targeted to the missing governed input rather than broad or generic.
No backend write or publish action should occur before clarification is resolved.
scenario.name: stop-for-clarification-when-required-analytics-context-is-missing
scenario.narrative: A user provides an incomplete request, such as asking a question without naming the dataset or requesting publication without a publish reason. The service pauses and asks for the missing governed input instead of inferring or defaulting silently.

orchestration_steps.action: User submits an incomplete analytics or publication request.
Service checks required inputs for the requested capability.
Service returns clarification_required with the minimum missing fields needed to proceed.
Execution remains paused until the user supplies the missing governed context.

scenario.time_scope: At request time before execution or approval routing

items.title: Stop for clarification when required analytics context is missing

items.body: An analytics creator turns governed metric and dimension intent into a chart preview. The default outcome is preview-only, with no saved chart unless a later approval-gated publication request is made.
items.rationale: The source defines chart preview creation with required inputs and explicitly states that charts are not saved by default.

scenario.actor_context: Analytics creator or owner preparing a visualization
scenario.business_scope: Preview-first chart creation under metric and dimension governance
expected_anip_support: Separate preview semantics from publication semantics in the contract.
Enforce preview-only defaults and bounded rendering options.
Audit preview generation without implying publication.
expected_behavior: Preview creation should remain non-persistent by default.
Only governed metrics, dimensions, and filters should be used.
The service should deny attempts to bypass preview posture and save directly.
scenario.name: create-a-chart-preview-without-saving-it
scenario.narrative: A creator wants to visualize an allowed metric from an allowed dataset. They request a chart preview using governed visualization intent, and the service produces a preview artifact without creating or updating a saved chart.

orchestration_steps.action: User provides dataset_ref, metric, and visualization_type, with optional dimension, time_window, filters, and title.
Service validates dataset visibility and metric and dimension governance.
Service generates a bounded chart preview using native Superset-backed rendering or chart-data behavior within policy limits.
Service returns a chart_preview_ref and preview output, but does not save a chart.

scenario.time_scope: Interactive design step before any publication request

items.title: Create a chart preview without saving it

items.body: After a chart preview is created, a creator requests that it be saved or updated in a dashboard scope. The service requires a preview reference and business reason, then stops at an approval boundary unless the actor is already authorized to approve.
items.rationale: The brief explicitly defines chart publish request inputs, approval gating, and approver roles of analytics owner or BI admin.

scenario.actor_context: Analytics creator requesting publication; analytics owner or BI admin as approver
scenario.business_scope: Approval-gated chart save or update workflow
expected_anip_support: Represent approval-required as a first-class governed outcome.
Bind publication only to approved, policy-compliant requests.
Maintain approval records and audit trace for the request and decision.
expected_behavior: Publication should never occur without a valid preview reference and publish reason.
Creators can prepare and request publication, but approval is required before persistence unless the actor is already authorized.
The service should make approval status explicit rather than implying a save already happened.
scenario.name: request-chart-publication-after-preview-with-approval-gating
scenario.narrative: A creator is satisfied with a chart preview and wants it saved or updated for broader use. The service packages the request with dashboard scope and reason, then routes it through the required approval boundary before any persistent publication occurs.

orchestration_steps.action: User submits chart_preview_ref, dashboard_scope, and reason, optionally proposing a title.
Service validates that the preview reference exists, the dashboard scope is governed, and the request includes a publish reason.
If the requester is not an approving actor, the service records an approval-required request and stops before publication.
If an authorized approver reviews and approves, the service can proceed to the native Superset save or update path within policy.

scenario.time_scope: After preview review and before persistent publication

items.title: Request chart publication after preview, with approval gating

items.body: An analytics creator assembles chart references into a dashboard draft aligned to an objective and audience. The service prepares a preview-oriented draft proposal and keeps publication outside the current step.
items.rationale: The source defines dashboard draft preparation clearly, but does not provide a separate explicit publish capability in this brief, so the scenario should stay at draft posture.

scenario.actor_context: Analytics creator or owner preparing dashboard structure
scenario.business_scope: Preview-only dashboard composition and update proposal
expected_anip_support: Support draft-oriented dashboard composition without exposing raw dashboard APIs directly to agents.
Preserve approval boundaries between draft preparation and any later publication step.
Audit draft preparation and review outcomes.
expected_behavior: Dashboard preparation should remain preview-only in this scenario.
The service should not bypass approval or hidden publication paths.
If dashboard scope is missing, the flow should stop for clarification.
scenario.name: prepare-a-dashboard-draft-or-update-proposal-without-publishing
scenario.narrative: A user wants to organize approved or previewed charts into a dashboard concept for a business objective. The service prepares a dashboard draft or update proposal, including layout hints and audience context, but does not publish it as part of this step.

orchestration_steps.action: User provides dashboard_scope, objective, and chart_refs, optionally adding layout_hint and audience.
Service validates dashboard scope and referenced charts against governed visibility and policy.
Service prepares a dashboard draft or update proposal for review.
Service returns the draft artifact and indicates that approval is required before any publish action.

scenario.time_scope: Planning and review stage before any publish decision

items.title: Prepare a dashboard draft or update proposal without publishing

items.body: A dataset draft requester proposes a virtual or semantic dataset using business intent, source tables, and metrics. The service prepares an approval-gated draft and explicitly refuses raw SQL as an agent input.
items.rationale: The source defines dataset draft preparation, approval-gated posture, and explicit denial of raw SQL from agents.

scenario.actor_context: Dataset draft requester proposing a new governed dataset
scenario.business_scope: Approval-gated semantic or virtual dataset drafting
expected_anip_support: Translate business dataset intent into a governed draft workflow rather than direct SQL tooling.
Enforce database, source-table, metric, and approval boundaries before any creation path.
Record audit-friendly draft and approval status.
expected_behavior: Raw SQL must be refused as an agent input.
Dataset creation should not occur automatically from the draft request.
Protected database access, allowlist bypass, and unapproved saved-query creation should be denied.
scenario.name: prepare-a-governed-dataset-draft-without-accepting-raw-sql
scenario.narrative: A requester needs a new governed dataset for future analytics use. Instead of supplying SQL, they describe the dataset purpose and query intent, and the service prepares a draft for approval within database and source-table governance.

orchestration_steps.action: User provides database_ref, dataset_purpose, and query_intent, optionally adding source_tables and metrics.
Service validates that the database and source scope are governed and that the request does not include raw SQL.
Service prepares a virtual or semantic dataset draft within policy constraints.
Service returns the draft as approval-gated work rather than creating the dataset immediately.

scenario.time_scope: Pre-implementation design stage before dataset creation

items.title: Prepare a governed dataset draft without accepting raw SQL

items.body: A user asks for raw SQL, a raw export, protected database access, or another bypass path. The service refuses the request and explains that only governed analytics capabilities are available.
items.rationale: The brief explicitly denies raw SQL, raw exports, protected database access, dashboard publication bypass, and similar unrestricted operations.

scenario.actor_context: Any actor attempting to use a disallowed analytics path
scenario.business_scope: Hard denial of non-governed analytics operations
expected_anip_support: Make denial rules part of the contract rather than relying on prompt-only guardrails.
Prevent backend mappings that would enable arbitrary SQL, unrestricted export, or allowlist bypass.
Capture denial outcomes for audit and governance review.
expected_behavior: The service should deny disallowed operations consistently across actors unless a separate approved capability exists.
No hidden fallback to raw backend tools should occur.
Denial reasons should be explicit enough for user understanding and audit review.
scenario.name: deny-requests-for-raw-sql-unrestricted-export-or-protected-access
scenario.narrative: A user attempts to step outside the governed analytics surface by asking for raw SQL, unrestricted export, or direct protected access. The service stops the request and returns a governed denial rather than trying to partially comply.

orchestration_steps.action: User requests a disallowed operation such as raw SQL, raw export, protected database access, or publication bypass.
Service evaluates the request against denial rules for the governed capability surface.
Service returns a denial with a concise reason and, where appropriate, points the user back to an allowed governed capability.

items.title: Deny requests for raw SQL, unrestricted export, or protected access

# Source 1: Superset governed fronting source specification
Kind: API docs
Filename: source-spec.md

# Superset Governed Fronting Showcase Source Specification

This source document models a realistic analytics fronting use case: Superset native APIs exist downstream, but agents should only see governed analytics capabilities.

## Purpose

Demonstrate that ANIP can govern analytics behavior in front of Apache Superset without exposing raw SQL, chart, dashboard, or dataset tools directly to agents.

The ANIP contract owns the analytics capability semantics. Superset native REST APIs are the durable backend for this showcase. Superset MCP may be useful as a comparison point for raw tool exposure, but this package does not use MCP as the ANIP execution interface.

## Service Boundary

- Service ID: `superset-governance-service`
- Service name: Superset Governance Service
- Primary backend: native Superset REST API adapter
- Deployment posture: centralized ANIP fronting service with enterprise SSO, Superset actor mapping, dataset allowlists, metric/dimension governance, SQL restrictions, approval records, and audit.

## Backend Evidence

Native Superset API supply:

- Dataset discovery and dataset metadata through `/api/v1/dataset/`.
- Chart discovery and chart metadata through `/api/v1/chart/`.
- Dashboard discovery and dashboard metadata through `/api/v1/dashboard/`.
- Chart-data, dataset, chart, and dashboard APIs where enabled by deployment policy.
- Chart/dashboard create or update APIs where enabled by deployment policy.

MCP comparison:

- Superset MCP exposes tool-level operations for datasets, charts, dashboards, SQL, and databases.
- This showcase intentionally does not bind ANIP capabilities to Superset MCP tools. The point is to expose a governed capability surface backed by bounded native APIs, not to rename raw MCP tools.
- ANIP adds business-level governance: metric allowlists, result-grain restrictions, preview-only defaults, explicit approvals, and denial of raw SQL or raw exports.

## Governed Capability Surface

| Capability | User intent | Required inputs | Optional governed inputs | Outcome posture |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `superset.analytics.discover_context` | Discover allowed datasets, charts, dashboards, metrics, and dimensions. | `workspace_scope`, `query` | `asset_type`, `limit`, `backend_options` | Read-only. Returns actor-visible analytics catalog summaries. |
| `superset.analytics.answer_question` | Answer a governed analytics question against allowed datasets and metrics. | `question`, `dataset_ref` | `metric`, `dimension`, `time_window`, `filters`, `limit`, `backend_options` | Read-only or preview. Uses bounded chart-data or provider-owned semantic execution; raw SQL is not an agent input or backend mapping. |
| `superset.chart.preview.create` | Create a chart preview from governed metric/dimension intent. | `dataset_ref`, `metric`, `visualization_type` | `dimension`, `time_window`, `filters`, `title`, `backend_options` | Preview-only. Does not save a chart by default. |
| `superset.chart.publish.request` | Request saving or updating a chart. | `chart_preview_ref`, `dashboard_scope`, `reason` | `title`, `backend_options` | Approval-gated. Requires analytics owner or BI admin approval. |
| `superset.dashboard.draft.prepare` | Prepare a dashboard draft or update proposal. | `dashboard_scope`, `objective`, `chart_refs` | `layout_hint`, `audience`, `backend_options` | Preview-only. Requires approval before publish. |
| `superset.dataset.draft.prepare` | Prepare a virtual dataset or semantic dataset draft. | `database_ref`, `dataset_purpose`, `query_intent` | `source_tables`, `metrics`, `backend_options` | Approval-gated draft. Raw SQL is not accepted from agents. |

`backend_options` is limited to bounded provider controls such as selected fields, pagination, chart rendering format, or preview output format. It must not allow arbitrary SQL execution, unbounded result export, database switching, dataset allowlist bypass, or hidden dashboard publication.

## Policy Semantics

- Dataset, chart, dashboard, and database scope must be actor-visible and allowlisted.
- Missing dataset, metric, chart preview, dashboard scope, or publish reason returns `clarification_required`.
- Chart creation defaults to preview-only. Saving charts, publishing dashboards, and creating virtual datasets require approval.
- Raw SQL is not accepted from agents. The service may use provider-owned semantic execution only within declared dataset, metric, grain, row-limit, and audit constraints.
- Owner-level, row-level, customer-level, or sensitive dimensions may be restricted even if Superset RBAC allows the underlying query.
- Raw exports, unrestricted SQL, protected database access, dashboard publication bypass, and unapproved saved-query creation are denied.
- Native REST APIs are the execution binding for this package. MCP can be used as a comparison surface, but is not required for ANIP.

## Why ANIP Helps

Superset MCP gives agents useful analytics tools, but it still exposes tool-level operations. ANIP exposes organization-approved analytics capabilities with explicit input semantics, metric constraints, preview behavior, approvals, denial rules, and audit.

## Source Notes

- Superset MCP admin documentation describes built-in MCP access for datasets, charts, dashboards, SQL, and databases, with RBAC enforcement and action-log audit: https://superset.apache.org/admin-docs/configuration/mcp-server/

Kind: Fronting intent
Filename: analytics-operations-fronting-intent.md

# Superset Fronting Showcase 0.2.0 Fronting Intent

Create a governed ANIP fronting service for Apache Superset. Agents should not receive raw Superset SQL, chart, dashboard, database, or MCP tools. They should receive governed analytics capabilities: discover allowed analytics context, answer bounded analytics questions, create chart previews, request chart publication, prepare dashboard drafts, and prepare dataset drafts. The backend integration is native Superset REST API. The ANIP contract must enforce dataset allowlists, metric and dimension constraints, preview-only defaults, approval for publish or dataset creation, denial of raw SQL and raw exports, clarification for missing dataset or publish context, and audit-friendly outcomes.

## Next review step
Add integration evidence from a real source before contract generation. Use uploaded OpenAPI, GraphQL, MCP schema, API docs, permission matrices, live discovery, or manual operation entry. Studio must not invent backend operations from the project name or domain.

data.approval_posture_summary: Discovery, bounded question answering, and chart preview can proceed within governed limits, but saving or updating charts, publishing dashboards, and creating datasets are approval-gated and must stop for analytics owner or BI admin review before execution.
business_goals: Expose organization-approved analytics capabilities instead of raw Superset or MCP tool operations.
Let agents answer analytics questions only within actor-visible, allowlisted dataset and metric boundaries.
Default exploratory analytics work to read-only or preview behavior.
Require explicit approval for chart publication, dashboard publication, and dataset creation workflows.
Deny raw SQL, raw exports, protected database access, and other bypass paths that would weaken governance.
Produce audit-friendly outcomes with clear clarification, denial, preview, and approval states.
data.business_problem: Native Superset APIs and MCP-style tool exposure can give agents access to low-level analytics operations, but the organization needs business-level governance over what analytics actions are visible, how questions are answered, when previews are allowed, and where approval, denial, and audit boundaries must stop higher-risk actions.
data.governed_behavior_summary: The product only exposes governed analytics capabilities backed by native Superset REST APIs. It limits work to actor-visible and allowlisted datasets, metrics, dimensions, and scopes; defaults chart creation and similar exploratory work to preview-only or read-only behavior; asks for clarification when required context is missing; denies raw SQL, raw exports, protected database access, and publication bypass; and keeps outcomes audit-friendly.

items.body: For superset.analytics.discover_context, superset.analytics.answer_question, superset.chart.preview.create, and superset.dashboard.draft.prepare, allow execution only when referenced workspace, dataset, chart, dashboard, metric, dimension, database, and table scope is actor-visible and allowlisted. Resolve entity references through the bounded catalogs named in the input contracts. If a reference is missing, ambiguous, invalid, or outside allowlist visibility, return clarification_required for missing or ambiguous governed inputs and deny for prohibited or unreachable scope. Do not expose raw Superset objects beyond governed summaries, draft artifacts, or bounded aggregate results.
items.rationale: The baseline repeatedly requires actor-visible allowlists, governed discovery, bounded answers, and read or draft posture. Input contracts and runtime governance already define catalog-based resolution and minimum scope.
items.title: Bind governed read capabilities to actor-visible allowlisted analytics scope

items.body: Return clarification_required instead of inferring missing governed inputs for any capability when required context is absent or unresolved. At minimum, require clarification for missing workspace_scope on discovery; question or dataset_ref on answer_question; dataset_ref, metric, or visualization_type on chart.preview.create; chart_preview_ref, dashboard_scope, or reason on chart.publish.request; dashboard_scope, objective, or chart_refs on dashboard.draft.prepare; and database_ref, dataset_purpose, or query_intent on dataset.draft.prepare. Clarification responses should name the exact missing governed input and use the contract hints where available.
items.rationale: The locked baseline explicitly calls out clarification_required for missing dataset, metric, chart preview, dashboard scope, and publish reason, and the completed input contracts define required fields and clarification hints.
items.title: Make clarification-required a first-class runtime outcome for incomplete governed requests

items.body: Create explicit deny bindings across all capabilities for requests that attempt raw SQL input, unrestricted export, protected database access, allowlist bypass, hidden dashboard publication, direct save without preview or approval, or unapproved saved-query creation. Denial should occur before backend execution and should produce an audit-friendly reason. Runtime policy must also reject backend_options or filters that contain SQL snippets, database switching intent, export intent, or references to restricted dimensions.
items.rationale: The baseline and denial scenario are explicit that these paths are prohibited and must be denied consistently rather than handled by prompt-only guardrails.
items.title: Deny raw SQL, unrestricted export, protected access, and hidden mutation paths

items.body: For answer_question and chart.preview.create, permit only governed metrics, dimensions, time windows, filters, and bounded provider options. Enforce default result limits from the input contracts unless a stricter runtime maximum is later specified. Filters must reference only allowed dimensions and must be denied when they target sensitive or restricted dimensions, even if downstream Superset RBAC would technically allow them. Runtime policy should preserve read-only or preview-only posture and prevent any mutation side effects during these executions.
items.rationale: The baseline requires bounded dataset and metric context, row limits, sensitive-dimension restrictions beyond backend RBAC, and preview-only defaults. Runtime governance marks these capabilities as read with no mutation outputs.
items.title: Constrain execution to bounded metrics, dimensions, filters, grain, and result limits

items.body: Bind superset.chart.preview.create and superset.dashboard.draft.prepare to non-persistent runtime posture. These capabilities may produce reviewable draft artifacts only and must not save, publish, or update Superset charts or dashboards as part of normal execution. Any request phrased as direct save or publish through these capabilities should be denied or redirected to the appropriate approval-gated path rather than silently mutating backend state.
items.rationale: The baseline states creation workflows default to preview-only outcomes, and runtime governance marks chart preview and dashboard draft as read or draft outputs that do not produce system mutation.
items.title: Enforce preview-only and draft-only defaults for non-persistent creation flows

items.body: For superset.chart.publish.request, require a valid governed chart_preview_ref, an allowed dashboard_scope, and a human-readable reason before any publish request can proceed. Runtime outcome should be approval_required or equivalent approval-request posture unless the final policy explicitly grants direct approval authority to the caller. The capability may prepare save or update intent and approval records, but must not execute create_chart or update_chart without a valid grant. Approval authority should be modeled as analytics owner or BI admin, consistent with the locked baseline.
items.rationale: The baseline and scenario explicitly require preview reference, dashboard scope, reason, and approval by analytics owner or BI admin before persistence. Runtime governance marks this capability as approval_gated with approval_required grant policy.
items.title: Bind chart publication to approval-required preview mutation posture

items.body: For superset.dataset.draft.prepare, require database_ref, dataset_purpose, and query_intent expressed in business terms. The runtime policy should allow optional source_tables and metrics only when they resolve to allowlisted references. Raw SQL must be denied as caller input. The capability may produce a governed dataset draft and approval request preview, but must not directly create a virtual or semantic dataset artifact without an explicit grant from the designated approver authority.
items.rationale: The baseline and source specification explicitly prohibit raw SQL input for dataset drafting and require approval before dataset creation. Runtime governance marks the capability as approval_gated and approval_required.
items.title: Bind dataset draft preparation to approval-gated provider-owned derivation without raw SQL

items.body: Across all capabilities, treat backend_options as optional bounded controls only for safe provider parameters such as selected fields, pagination, rendering format, preview output format, or draft metadata. Deny any backend_options payload that attempts arbitrary SQL execution, unbounded export, database switching, hidden publication, arbitrary mutation payloads, or allowlist bypass. Where backend_options is omitted, preserve the contract default behavior rather than inferring broader backend access.
items.rationale: The source specification and completed input contracts repeatedly define backend_options as bounded and explicitly prohibit SQL, export, database switching, and hidden mutation behavior.
items.title: Restrict backend_options to enumerably safe provider controls

items.body: Attach durable, searchable audit records to discovery, bounded answers, previews, dashboard drafts, approval requests, denials, and clarification stops. Each record should preserve task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id when present, plus the requested capability, resolved governed scope, resulting posture, and the policy reason for allow, deny, clarification, or approval stop. Signed trust mode should be preserved for runtime decisions and approval records.
items.rationale: The baseline requires durable searchable audit, lineage identifiers, signed trust mode, and audit-friendly outcomes that explain what was requested, what boundary applied, and why the result occurred.
items.title: Require durable audit and lineage on governed actions, denials, approvals, and clarification stops

Should dataset draft approval use the same approver set as chart publication, or a narrower database or data-governance approver group?
Do you want explicit numeric maximums beyond the current defaults for discovery limit 25 and answer-question limit 50, or should runtime policy only preserve those defaults?
Should dashboard draft preparation accept only saved chart references, or also governed chart preview references when composing a draft proposal?
Do you want runtime policy to explicitly classify restricted dimensions by category such as owner-level, row-level, customer-level, or keep that as a generic sensitive-dimension restriction?

watchouts: The baseline says dashboard publication requires approval, but no separate dashboard publish capability exists in the current capability inventory; policy should avoid inventing one here.
All six capabilities are atomic at the ANIP layer; runtime policy should not imply child ANIP compositions for native Superset REST calls.
Approval-gated capabilities stop at approval request or draft posture and must not imply that backend create or update already occurred.
backend_options must stay tightly bounded and must not open raw SQL, export, database switching, allowlist bypass, or hidden publication paths.
Clarification-required and denial are distinct outcomes and should not be collapsed into a single generic error posture.

items.body: Implement the Superset Governance Service against native Superset REST endpoints as the durable backend binding. Use dataset, chart, dashboard, chart-data, and related REST APIs where enabled by deployment policy. Do not expose or depend on Superset MCP as the execution interface, and do not model raw SQL execution as a backend binding path.
items.rationale: The locked baseline and source specification explicitly require native Superset REST APIs as the backend execution binding and reject MCP and raw SQL as the governed contract surface.
items.title: Bind the service to native Superset REST as the only execution backend

items.body: Bind each governed capability as a single service-level operation implemented by internal adapter logic rather than ANIP child composition. The adapter may call multiple Superset REST endpoints internally for discovery, preview, or validation, but the public contract remains six atomic capabilities only: discover context, answer question, chart preview, chart publish request, dashboard draft prepare, and dataset draft prepare.
items.rationale: Completed composition evidence marks all six capabilities as atomic and states that native Superset REST calls are provider-owned internals, not child composition steps.
items.title: Keep capability-to-backend mapping atomic inside one provider-owned adapter

items.body: Map discover_context to read-only Superset catalog and metadata retrieval for datasets, charts, and dashboards, filtered by workspace and allowlists. Map answer_question to bounded chart-data style reads or provider-owned semantic execution over an allowed dataset. Do not permit raw exports, unrestricted result retrieval, or any mutation-capable backend path from these capabilities.
items.rationale: Runtime governance defines these capabilities as read operations with explicit non-production of raw export or mutation outcomes.
items.title: Bind discovery and question answering to bounded read paths only

items.body: Implement chart.preview.create through a preview-only adapter path that generates a reviewable chart artifact from governed dataset, metric, dimension, filter, and visualization inputs. The binding may use Superset chart-data preview behavior but must not create or update a saved chart record as part of preview generation.
items.rationale: The baseline and runtime governance explicitly state that chart preview is non-persistent and does not save or publish a chart.
items.title: Bind chart preview to unsaved chart-data preview execution

items.body: Implement chart.publish.request as an approval-gated binding that validates chart_preview_ref, dashboard_scope, and reason, then creates an approval request or preview-mutation record. Do not call Superset chart create/update endpoints until an authorized approval grant exists. If direct approval execution is added later, keep it as a separate controlled backend path with full audit trace.
items.rationale: Runtime governance marks this capability as approval_required and says backend save/update is not executed without a valid grant.
items.title: Bind chart publication requests to approval-preview records before any save/update call

items.body: Implement dashboard.draft.prepare as a preview-only binding that resolves dashboard scope, validates chart references, and assembles a reviewable dashboard draft or update proposal. The adapter may read existing dashboard metadata where useful, but it must not publish or mutate dashboard state through Superset as part of this capability.
items.rationale: The locked scenario and runtime governance both define dashboard drafting as preview-only and explicitly outside publication.
items.title: Bind dashboard draft preparation to non-publishing draft assembly

items.body: Implement dataset.draft.prepare as an approval-gated draft flow that accepts business-purpose and query-intent inputs, optionally resolves approved source tables and metrics, and produces a reviewable dataset draft. The adapter may inspect approved databases and metadata, but callers must never supply raw SQL and the capability must not directly create a Superset dataset before approval.
items.rationale: The baseline, source spec, and runtime governance all state that raw SQL is not accepted and dataset draft preparation stops at approval-preview posture.
items.title: Bind dataset draft preparation to provider-owned derivation without raw SQL input

items.body: Use the service's delegated auth posture to map enterprise SSO identities to Superset actors before issuing downstream REST calls. Preserve scoped authority, purpose binding, and lineage identifiers on every backend invocation so audit records can explain who requested what, under which governed capability, and against which downstream actor context.
items.rationale: The locked baseline requires purpose binding, scoped authority, delegation tokens, and lineage fields, and the service responsibilities explicitly mention enterprise SSO identity mapping to Superset actors.
items.title: Bind downstream calls through delegated identity with Superset actor mapping

items.body: Insert a policy enforcement layer ahead of adapter calls to resolve and validate workspace scope, dataset references, dashboard scope, database references, metrics, dimensions, filters, and backend_options. Deny or clarify before backend execution when inputs are missing, ambiguous, restricted, or outside allowlists. Sensitive dimensions must remain blockable even if Superset RBAC would technically allow access.
items.rationale: The product baseline repeatedly states that governance rules take precedence over backend reachability and that clarification/denial must happen before execution.
items.title: Enforce allowlists and sensitive-dimension policy before any Superset REST invocation

items.body: Do not pass backend_options through directly to Superset payloads. Translate only approved controls such as selected fields, pagination, rendering format, preview format, or metadata-only options into downstream REST parameters. Reject any option that could imply arbitrary SQL, export, database switching, allowlist bypass, hidden publication, or unrestricted mutation.
items.rationale: The source specification and input contracts explicitly constrain backend_options and warn against arbitrary passthrough behavior.
items.title: Bind backend_options through an explicit allowlist translator

items.body: Persist audit-friendly records for discovery, answers, previews, clarification stops, denials, approval requests, and later approval decisions in a durable searchable store controlled by the service. Include task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, parent_invocation_id, actor identity context, resolved asset references, applied governance boundary, and resulting posture.
items.rationale: The locked baseline requires durable searchable audit, lineage preservation, and explicit audit-friendly outcomes across governed actions and denials.
items.title: Bind governed outcomes to durable searchable audit storage

questions_for_user: Which Superset deployment(s) should this service bind to first: production, staging, or both?
How should enterprise SSO identities be mapped to Superset actors for downstream REST calls?
Should approval requests remain internal-only records at first, or integrate with an external approval system?
Which native Superset REST endpoints are enabled in the target deployment for chart create/update and dataset-related operations?
What concrete allowlist sources should drive dataset, dashboard, chart, database, metric, and dimension visibility?

envelope.title: Backend Binding Proposal: Superset Governance Service
watchouts: All six capabilities are atomic at the ANIP layer; native Superset REST calls stay internal adapter behavior, not child capability composition.
Do not bind any capability to Superset MCP or raw SQL execution paths.
Preview and draft capabilities must not persist Superset assets by default.
Approval-gated capabilities must stop at approval request posture unless a valid grant path is explicitly implemented.
backend_options must stay tightly allowlisted and must not enable export, database switching, arbitrary payload passthrough, or hidden publication.
Governance restrictions may be stricter than downstream Superset RBAC and must win on conflict.

items.body: Verification should prove that `superset.analytics.discover_context`, `superset.analytics.answer_question`, and `superset.chart.preview.create` execute as bounded read or preview operations only. Checks should confirm actor-visible allowlists are enforced before backend execution, required references resolve through the governed catalogs, default limits are applied when omitted, and outputs remain summary, aggregate, or draft-oriented rather than raw backend payloads. Verification should also confirm that question answering and chart preview never accept raw SQL input and never expose raw SQL in outputs.
items.rationale: The baseline and runtime governance explicitly define these capabilities as atomic bounded reads or previews with no raw SQL exposure and no mutation side effects.
items.title: Verify bounded read behavior for discovery, question answering, and chart preview

items.body: Verification should prove that `superset.chart.publish.request` and `superset.dataset.draft.prepare` produce approval-request or approval-preview outcomes without executing persistent Superset mutations by default. Checks should require valid governed references and reasons, confirm missing required inputs return clarification-required, and confirm no chart save, chart update, dataset creation, or hidden publish occurs before an explicit grant path exists. Evidence should show the returned outcome makes pending approval status explicit rather than implying the mutation already happened.
items.rationale: The locked baseline and runtime governance define both capabilities as approval-gated and explicitly state execution is not performed without a valid grant.
items.title: Verify approval-gated capabilities stop at request posture before persistence

items.body: Verification should prove that `superset.chart.preview.create` and `superset.dashboard.draft.prepare` remain non-persistent by default. Checks should confirm chart preview returns a reviewable preview artifact, dashboard draft returns a reviewable draft or update proposal, and neither capability saves, publishes, or mutates Superset assets as part of normal execution. Verification should also confirm attempts to use these capabilities as hidden save or publish paths are denied or redirected to the proper approval-gated flow.
items.rationale: Preview-first and draft-only posture is a core product boundary and is repeated across the baseline, scenarios, source spec, and runtime governance evidence.
items.title: Verify preview-only and draft-only non-persistence semantics

items.body: Verification should prove the service returns structured clarification-required outcomes when required governed inputs are missing, ambiguous, or unresolved. Coverage should include at least missing `workspace_scope`, `question`, `dataset_ref`, `metric` for chart preview, `chart_preview_ref`, `dashboard_scope`, `reason`, `objective`, `chart_refs`, `database_ref`, `dataset_purpose`, and `query_intent` where applicable. Checks should confirm the clarification message identifies the specific missing governed input and that no backend write or approval execution proceeds before clarification is resolved.
items.rationale: The baseline explicitly calls clarification-required a required posture, and the input contracts define clarify behavior for missing or unresolved required inputs.
items.title: Verify targeted clarification-required outcomes for missing governed inputs

items.body: Verification should include a denial matrix covering raw SQL requests, unrestricted export attempts, protected database access, allowlist bypass, hidden publication behavior, unapproved saved-query creation, restricted-dimension filters, and invalid backend options. Checks should confirm denials are explicit, audit-friendly, and consistent across capabilities, with no fallback to raw Superset tools, MCP-style operations, or unrestricted backend execution. Verification should also confirm unresolved closed-value inputs and prohibited backend option payloads are denied where the contracts require denial.
items.rationale: Denial of non-governed behavior is a central product promise and is reinforced by scenarios, policy semantics, and input contract resolution rules.
items.title: Verify explicit denial of prohibited analytics and bypass behaviors

items.body: Verification should prove that dataset, dashboard, chart, database, workspace, metric, dimension, preview, and table references are resolved through the intended governed catalogs and are constrained to actor-visible allowlisted assets. Checks should confirm ambiguous or unresolved references do not fall through to backend guesses, and that sensitive or restricted dimensions can still be blocked even when downstream Superset RBAC would technically allow access. Evidence should show policy enforcement occurs before provider-owned REST execution.
items.rationale: The service boundary is defined around governance taking precedence over raw backend reachability, with backend-resolved references and allowlist enforcement called out repeatedly.
items.title: Verify governed reference resolution and allowlist enforcement before backend calls

items.body: Verification should prove that optional controls such as `limit`, `asset_type`, `visualization_type`, `audience`, and `backend_options` behave exactly within contract bounds. Checks should confirm documented defaults are applied when omitted, closed-value fields reject unsupported values, and backend options permit only bounded provider controls such as selected fields, pagination, rendering format, preview format, or metadata-only options. Verification should explicitly reject arbitrary SQL execution, database switching, unbounded export, hidden publication, and arbitrary mutation payloads through backend options.
items.rationale: The source spec and input contracts define bounded provider controls as a major governance boundary and specify default, clarify, or deny behavior for these inputs.
items.title: Verify bounded backend options, defaults, and closed-value controls

items.body: Verification should prove that discovery, bounded answers, previews, drafts, approval requests, denials, and clarification stops all emit durable, searchable, audit-friendly records. Checks should confirm records preserve enough context to explain what was requested, what governance boundary applied, and why the resulting posture was returned. Where implemented, verification should also assert presence of lineage identifiers such as task ID, invocation ID, client reference ID, and parent invocation ID so outcomes can be traced across requests.
items.rationale: Audit durability, searchability, and lineage are explicit baseline requirements, but exact record schemas are not fully specified in the supplied evidence.
items.title: Verify audit-friendly records and lineage across success, denial, approval, and clarification outcomes

items.body: Verification should confirm that each of the six governed capabilities is treated as atomic at the ANIP contract layer and does not expose child capability composition, raw Superset REST operations, or MCP-style tool surfaces to callers. Checks should validate that any native Superset REST calls remain internal adapter behavior and that external verification focuses on governed inputs, governed outcomes, and policy enforcement rather than internal orchestration leakage.
items.rationale: Completed developer evidence explicitly marks all six capabilities as atomic and states native Superset REST calls are provider-owned internals, not ANIP child steps.
items.title: Verify atomic ANIP capability behavior and provider-owned adapter internals

questions_for_user: Should verification require live Superset REST evidence for every capability, or are mocked provider responses acceptable for some paths?
Do you want approval-gated capabilities verified only up to approval request creation, or also through a separate approver-grant execution path outside this contract pass?
Should audit verification require exact field-level assertions for lineage identifiers and denial reasons, or only presence and searchability checks?
Do you want negative-path verification to cover every prohibited behavior listed in policy, or a representative denial matrix?
envelope.summary: Candidate developer-facing verification expectation blocks derived from the locked Superset governed fronting baseline and completed developer evidence. Focus is on proving bounded analytics behavior, approval posture, clarification stops, denial rules, and atomic capability execution without exposing raw Superset tools or SQL.
envelope.title: Verification Expectations Proposal: Superset Fronting Showcase 0.2.0
watchouts: The baseline makes all six capabilities atomic from the ANIP contract perspective, so verification should not assume child capability composition.
Preview and draft capabilities must not be verified as persistent writes; success is a reviewable artifact, not a saved Superset object.
Approval-gated capabilities stop at approval request or preview-mutation posture unless a separate granted execution path is explicitly added later.
Raw SQL, unrestricted export, protected database access, allowlist bypass, and hidden publication must be verified as explicit denials, not silent failures.
Clarification-required behavior is part of the contract and should be verified as a first-class outcome, not treated as generic validation noise.
Backend options are intentionally bounded; verification should prove disallowed options are rejected rather than passed through.

accepted_payload.body: For superset.analytics.discover_context, superset.analytics.answer_question, superset.chart.preview.create, and superset.dashboard.draft.prepare, allow execution only when referenced workspace, dataset, chart, dashboard, metric, dimension, database, and table scope is actor-visible and allowlisted. Resolve entity references through the bounded catalogs named in the input contracts. If a reference is missing, ambiguous, invalid, or outside allowlist visibility, return clarification_required for missing or ambiguous governed inputs and deny for prohibited or unreachable scope. Do not expose raw Superset objects beyond governed summaries, draft artifacts, or bounded aggregate results.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline repeatedly requires actor-visible allowlists, governed discovery, bounded answers, and read or draft posture. Input contracts and runtime governance already define catalog-based resolution and minimum scope.
accepted_payload.title: Bind governed read capabilities to actor-visible allowlisted analytics scope

accepted_payload.body: Return clarification_required instead of inferring missing governed inputs for any capability when required context is absent or unresolved. At minimum, require clarification for missing workspace_scope on discovery; question or dataset_ref on answer_question; dataset_ref, metric, or visualization_type on chart.preview.create; chart_preview_ref, dashboard_scope, or reason on chart.publish.request; dashboard_scope, objective, or chart_refs on dashboard.draft.prepare; and database_ref, dataset_purpose, or query_intent on dataset.draft.prepare. Clarification responses should name the exact missing governed input and use the contract hints where available.
accepted_payload.rationale: The locked baseline explicitly calls out clarification_required for missing dataset, metric, chart preview, dashboard scope, and publish reason, and the completed input contracts define required fields and clarification hints.
accepted_payload.title: Make clarification-required a first-class runtime outcome for incomplete governed requests

accepted_payload.body: Create explicit deny bindings across all capabilities for requests that attempt raw SQL input, unrestricted export, protected database access, allowlist bypass, hidden dashboard publication, direct save without preview or approval, or unapproved saved-query creation. Denial should occur before backend execution and should produce an audit-friendly reason. Runtime policy must also reject backend_options or filters that contain SQL snippets, database switching intent, export intent, or references to restricted dimensions.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline and denial scenario are explicit that these paths are prohibited and must be denied consistently rather than handled by prompt-only guardrails.
accepted_payload.title: Deny raw SQL, unrestricted export, protected access, and hidden mutation paths

accepted_payload.body: For answer_question and chart.preview.create, permit only governed metrics, dimensions, time windows, filters, and bounded provider options. Enforce default result limits from the input contracts unless a stricter runtime maximum is later specified. Filters must reference only allowed dimensions and must be denied when they target sensitive or restricted dimensions, even if downstream Superset RBAC would technically allow them. Runtime policy should preserve read-only or preview-only posture and prevent any mutation side effects during these executions.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline requires bounded dataset and metric context, row limits, sensitive-dimension restrictions beyond backend RBAC, and preview-only defaults. Runtime governance marks these capabilities as read with no mutation outputs.
accepted_payload.title: Constrain execution to bounded metrics, dimensions, filters, grain, and result limits

accepted_payload.body: Bind superset.chart.preview.create and superset.dashboard.draft.prepare to non-persistent runtime posture. These capabilities may produce reviewable draft artifacts only and must not save, publish, or update Superset charts or dashboards as part of normal execution. Any request phrased as direct save or publish through these capabilities should be denied or redirected to the appropriate approval-gated path rather than silently mutating backend state.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline states creation workflows default to preview-only outcomes, and runtime governance marks chart preview and dashboard draft as read or draft outputs that do not produce system mutation.
accepted_payload.title: Enforce preview-only and draft-only defaults for non-persistent creation flows

accepted_payload.body: For superset.chart.publish.request, require a valid governed chart_preview_ref, an allowed dashboard_scope, and a human-readable reason before any publish request can proceed. Runtime outcome should be approval_required or equivalent approval-request posture unless the final policy explicitly grants direct approval authority to the caller. The capability may prepare save or update intent and approval records, but must not execute create_chart or update_chart without a valid grant. Approval authority should be modeled as analytics owner or BI admin, consistent with the locked baseline.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline and scenario explicitly require preview reference, dashboard scope, reason, and approval by analytics owner or BI admin before persistence. Runtime governance marks this capability as approval_gated with approval_required grant policy.
accepted_payload.title: Bind chart publication to approval-required preview mutation posture

accepted_payload.body: For superset.dataset.draft.prepare, require database_ref, dataset_purpose, and query_intent expressed in business terms. The runtime policy should allow optional source_tables and metrics only when they resolve to allowlisted references. Raw SQL must be denied as caller input. The capability may produce a governed dataset draft and approval request preview, but must not directly create a virtual or semantic dataset artifact without an explicit grant from the designated approver authority.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline and source specification explicitly prohibit raw SQL input for dataset drafting and require approval before dataset creation. Runtime governance marks the capability as approval_gated and approval_required.
accepted_payload.title: Bind dataset draft preparation to approval-gated provider-owned derivation without raw SQL

accepted_payload.body: Across all capabilities, treat backend_options as optional bounded controls only for safe provider parameters such as selected fields, pagination, rendering format, preview output format, or draft metadata. Deny any backend_options payload that attempts arbitrary SQL execution, unbounded export, database switching, hidden publication, arbitrary mutation payloads, or allowlist bypass. Where backend_options is omitted, preserve the contract default behavior rather than inferring broader backend access.
accepted_payload.rationale: The source specification and completed input contracts repeatedly define backend_options as bounded and explicitly prohibit SQL, export, database switching, and hidden mutation behavior.
accepted_payload.title: Restrict backend_options to enumerably safe provider controls

accepted_payload.body: Attach durable, searchable audit records to discovery, bounded answers, previews, dashboard drafts, approval requests, denials, and clarification stops. Each record should preserve task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id when present, plus the requested capability, resolved governed scope, resulting posture, and the policy reason for allow, deny, clarification, or approval stop. Signed trust mode should be preserved for runtime decisions and approval records.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline requires durable searchable audit, lineage identifiers, signed trust mode, and audit-friendly outcomes that explain what was requested, what boundary applied, and why the result occurred.
accepted_payload.title: Require durable audit and lineage on governed actions, denials, approvals, and clarification stops
