items.body: For answer_question and chart.preview.create, permit only governed metrics, dimensions, time windows, filters, and bounded provider options. Enforce default result limits from the input contracts unless a stricter runtime maximum is later specified. Filters must reference only allowed dimensions and must be denied when they target sensitive or restricted dimensions, even if downstream Superset RBAC would technically allow them. Runtime policy should preserve read-only or preview-only posture and prevent any mutation side effects during these executions.
items.rationale: The baseline requires bounded dataset and metric context, row limits, sensitive-dimension restrictions beyond backend RBAC, and preview-only defaults. Runtime governance marks these capabilities as read with no mutation outputs.
items.title: Constrain execution to bounded metrics, dimensions, filters, grain, and result limits

items.body: Bind superset.chart.preview.create and superset.dashboard.draft.prepare to non-persistent runtime posture. These capabilities may produce reviewable draft artifacts only and must not save, publish, or update Superset charts or dashboards as part of normal execution. Any request phrased as direct save or publish through these capabilities should be denied or redirected to the appropriate approval-gated path rather than silently mutating backend state.
items.rationale: The baseline states creation workflows default to preview-only outcomes, and runtime governance marks chart preview and dashboard draft as read or draft outputs that do not produce system mutation.
items.title: Enforce preview-only and draft-only defaults for non-persistent creation flows

items.body: For superset.chart.publish.request, require a valid governed chart_preview_ref, an allowed dashboard_scope, and a human-readable reason before any publish request can proceed. Runtime outcome should be approval_required or equivalent approval-request posture unless the final policy explicitly grants direct approval authority to the caller. The capability may prepare save or update intent and approval records, but must not execute create_chart or update_chart without a valid grant. Approval authority should be modeled as analytics owner or BI admin, consistent with the locked baseline.
items.rationale: The baseline and scenario explicitly require preview reference, dashboard scope, reason, and approval by analytics owner or BI admin before persistence. Runtime governance marks this capability as approval_gated with approval_required grant policy.
items.title: Bind chart publication to approval-required preview mutation posture

items.body: For superset.dataset.draft.prepare, require database_ref, dataset_purpose, and query_intent expressed in business terms. The runtime policy should allow optional source_tables and metrics only when they resolve to allowlisted references. Raw SQL must be denied as caller input. The capability may produce a governed dataset draft and approval request preview, but must not directly create a virtual or semantic dataset artifact without an explicit grant from the designated approver authority.
items.rationale: The baseline and source specification explicitly prohibit raw SQL input for dataset drafting and require approval before dataset creation. Runtime governance marks the capability as approval_gated and approval_required.
items.title: Bind dataset draft preparation to approval-gated provider-owned derivation without raw SQL

items.body: Across all capabilities, treat backend_options as optional bounded controls only for safe provider parameters such as selected fields, pagination, rendering format, preview output format, or draft metadata. Deny any backend_options payload that attempts arbitrary SQL execution, unbounded export, database switching, hidden publication, arbitrary mutation payloads, or allowlist bypass. Where backend_options is omitted, preserve the contract default behavior rather than inferring broader backend access.
items.rationale: The source specification and completed input contracts repeatedly define backend_options as bounded and explicitly prohibit SQL, export, database switching, and hidden mutation behavior.
items.title: Restrict backend_options to enumerably safe provider controls

items.body: Attach durable, searchable audit records to discovery, bounded answers, previews, dashboard drafts, approval requests, denials, and clarification stops. Each record should preserve task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id when present, plus the requested capability, resolved governed scope, resulting posture, and the policy reason for allow, deny, clarification, or approval stop. Signed trust mode should be preserved for runtime decisions and approval records.
items.rationale: The baseline requires durable searchable audit, lineage identifiers, signed trust mode, and audit-friendly outcomes that explain what was requested, what boundary applied, and why the result occurred.
items.title: Require durable audit and lineage on governed actions, denials, approvals, and clarification stops

pm_artifacts.title: Runtime Policy Binding Proposal: Superset Fronting Showcase 0.2.0

accepted_payload.body: Implement the Superset Governance Service against native Superset REST endpoints as the durable backend binding. Use dataset, chart, dashboard, chart-data, and related REST APIs where enabled by deployment policy. Do not expose or depend on Superset MCP as the execution interface, and do not model raw SQL execution as a backend binding path.
accepted_payload.rationale: The locked baseline and source specification explicitly require native Superset REST APIs as the backend execution binding and reject MCP and raw SQL as the governed contract surface.
accepted_payload.title: Bind the service to native Superset REST as the only execution backend

accepted_payload.body: Bind each governed capability as a single service-level operation implemented by internal adapter logic rather than ANIP child composition. The adapter may call multiple Superset REST endpoints internally for discovery, preview, or validation, but the public contract remains six atomic capabilities only: discover context, answer question, chart preview, chart publish request, dashboard draft prepare, and dataset draft prepare.
accepted_payload.rationale: Completed composition evidence marks all six capabilities as atomic and states that native Superset REST calls are provider-owned internals, not child composition steps.
accepted_payload.title: Keep capability-to-backend mapping atomic inside one provider-owned adapter

accepted_payload.body: Map discover_context to read-only Superset catalog and metadata retrieval for datasets, charts, and dashboards, filtered by workspace and allowlists. Map answer_question to bounded chart-data style reads or provider-owned semantic execution over an allowed dataset. Do not permit raw exports, unrestricted result retrieval, or any mutation-capable backend path from these capabilities.
accepted_payload.rationale: Runtime governance defines these capabilities as read operations with explicit non-production of raw export or mutation outcomes.
accepted_payload.title: Bind discovery and question answering to bounded read paths only

accepted_payload.body: Implement chart.preview.create through a preview-only adapter path that generates a reviewable chart artifact from governed dataset, metric, dimension, filter, and visualization inputs. The binding may use Superset chart-data preview behavior but must not create or update a saved chart record as part of preview generation.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline and runtime governance explicitly state that chart preview is non-persistent and does not save or publish a chart.
accepted_payload.title: Bind chart preview to unsaved chart-data preview execution

accepted_payload.body: Implement chart.publish.request as an approval-gated binding that validates chart_preview_ref, dashboard_scope, and reason, then creates an approval request or preview-mutation record. Do not call Superset chart create/update endpoints until an authorized approval grant exists. If direct approval execution is added later, keep it as a separate controlled backend path with full audit trace.
accepted_payload.rationale: Runtime governance marks this capability as approval_required and says backend save/update is not executed without a valid grant.
accepted_payload.title: Bind chart publication requests to approval-preview records before any save/update call

accepted_payload.body: Implement dashboard.draft.prepare as a preview-only binding that resolves dashboard scope, validates chart references, and assembles a reviewable dashboard draft or update proposal. The adapter may read existing dashboard metadata where useful, but it must not publish or mutate dashboard state through Superset as part of this capability.
accepted_payload.rationale: The locked scenario and runtime governance both define dashboard drafting as preview-only and explicitly outside publication.
accepted_payload.title: Bind dashboard draft preparation to non-publishing draft assembly

accepted_payload.body: Implement dataset.draft.prepare as an approval-gated draft flow that accepts business-purpose and query-intent inputs, optionally resolves approved source tables and metrics, and produces a reviewable dataset draft. The adapter may inspect approved databases and metadata, but callers must never supply raw SQL and the capability must not directly create a Superset dataset before approval.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline, source spec, and runtime governance all state that raw SQL is not accepted and dataset draft preparation stops at approval-preview posture.
accepted_payload.title: Bind dataset draft preparation to provider-owned derivation without raw SQL input

accepted_payload.body: Use the service's delegated auth posture to map enterprise SSO identities to Superset actors before issuing downstream REST calls. Preserve scoped authority, purpose binding, and lineage identifiers on every backend invocation so audit records can explain who requested what, under which governed capability, and against which downstream actor context.
accepted_payload.rationale: The locked baseline requires purpose binding, scoped authority, delegation tokens, and lineage fields, and the service responsibilities explicitly mention enterprise SSO identity mapping to Superset actors.
accepted_payload.title: Bind downstream calls through delegated identity with Superset actor mapping

accepted_payload.body: Insert a policy enforcement layer ahead of adapter calls to resolve and validate workspace scope, dataset references, dashboard scope, database references, metrics, dimensions, filters, and backend_options. Deny or clarify before backend execution when inputs are missing, ambiguous, restricted, or outside allowlists. Sensitive dimensions must remain blockable even if Superset RBAC would technically allow access.
accepted_payload.rationale: The product baseline repeatedly states that governance rules take precedence over backend reachability and that clarification/denial must happen before execution.
accepted_payload.title: Enforce allowlists and sensitive-dimension policy before any Superset REST invocation

accepted_payload.body: Do not pass backend_options through directly to Superset payloads. Translate only approved controls such as selected fields, pagination, rendering format, preview format, or metadata-only options into downstream REST parameters. Reject any option that could imply arbitrary SQL, export, database switching, allowlist bypass, hidden publication, or unrestricted mutation.
accepted_payload.rationale: The source specification and input contracts explicitly constrain backend_options and warn against arbitrary passthrough behavior.
accepted_payload.title: Bind backend_options through an explicit allowlist translator

accepted_payload.body: Persist audit-friendly records for discovery, answers, previews, clarification stops, denials, approval requests, and later approval decisions in a durable searchable store controlled by the service. Include task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, parent_invocation_id, actor identity context, resolved asset references, applied governance boundary, and resulting posture.
accepted_payload.rationale: The locked baseline requires durable searchable audit, lineage preservation, and explicit audit-friendly outcomes across governed actions and denials.
accepted_payload.title: Bind governed outcomes to durable searchable audit storage

pm_artifacts.title: Backend Binding Proposal: Superset Governance Service

accepted_payload.body: Verification should prove that `superset.analytics.discover_context`, `superset.analytics.answer_question`, and `superset.chart.preview.create` execute as bounded read or preview operations only. Checks should confirm actor-visible allowlists are enforced before backend execution, required references resolve through the governed catalogs, default limits are applied when omitted, and outputs remain summary, aggregate, or draft-oriented rather than raw backend payloads. Verification should also confirm that question answering and chart preview never accept raw SQL input and never expose raw SQL in outputs.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline and runtime governance explicitly define these capabilities as atomic bounded reads or previews with no raw SQL exposure and no mutation side effects.
accepted_payload.title: Verify bounded read behavior for discovery, question answering, and chart preview

accepted_payload.body: Verification should prove that `superset.chart.publish.request` and `superset.dataset.draft.prepare` produce approval-request or approval-preview outcomes without executing persistent Superset mutations by default. Checks should require valid governed references and reasons, confirm missing required inputs return clarification-required, and confirm no chart save, chart update, dataset creation, or hidden publish occurs before an explicit grant path exists. Evidence should show the returned outcome makes pending approval status explicit rather than implying the mutation already happened.
accepted_payload.rationale: The locked baseline and runtime governance define both capabilities as approval-gated and explicitly state execution is not performed without a valid grant.
accepted_payload.title: Verify approval-gated capabilities stop at request posture before persistence

accepted_payload.body: Verification should prove that `superset.chart.preview.create` and `superset.dashboard.draft.prepare` remain non-persistent by default. Checks should confirm chart preview returns a reviewable preview artifact, dashboard draft returns a reviewable draft or update proposal, and neither capability saves, publishes, or mutates Superset assets as part of normal execution. Verification should also confirm attempts to use these capabilities as hidden save or publish paths are denied or redirected to the proper approval-gated flow.
accepted_payload.rationale: Preview-first and draft-only posture is a core product boundary and is repeated across the baseline, scenarios, source spec, and runtime governance evidence.
accepted_payload.title: Verify preview-only and draft-only non-persistence semantics

accepted_payload.body: Verification should prove the service returns structured clarification-required outcomes when required governed inputs are missing, ambiguous, or unresolved. Coverage should include at least missing `workspace_scope`, `question`, `dataset_ref`, `metric` for chart preview, `chart_preview_ref`, `dashboard_scope`, `reason`, `objective`, `chart_refs`, `database_ref`, `dataset_purpose`, and `query_intent` where applicable. Checks should confirm the clarification message identifies the specific missing governed input and that no backend write or approval execution proceeds before clarification is resolved.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline explicitly calls clarification-required a required posture, and the input contracts define clarify behavior for missing or unresolved required inputs.
accepted_payload.title: Verify targeted clarification-required outcomes for missing governed inputs

accepted_payload.body: Verification should include a denial matrix covering raw SQL requests, unrestricted export attempts, protected database access, allowlist bypass, hidden publication behavior, unapproved saved-query creation, restricted-dimension filters, and invalid backend options. Checks should confirm denials are explicit, audit-friendly, and consistent across capabilities, with no fallback to raw Superset tools, MCP-style operations, or unrestricted backend execution. Verification should also confirm unresolved closed-value inputs and prohibited backend option payloads are denied where the contracts require denial.
accepted_payload.rationale: Denial of non-governed behavior is a central product promise and is reinforced by scenarios, policy semantics, and input contract resolution rules.
accepted_payload.title: Verify explicit denial of prohibited analytics and bypass behaviors

accepted_payload.body: Verification should prove that dataset, dashboard, chart, database, workspace, metric, dimension, preview, and table references are resolved through the intended governed catalogs and are constrained to actor-visible allowlisted assets. Checks should confirm ambiguous or unresolved references do not fall through to backend guesses, and that sensitive or restricted dimensions can still be blocked even when downstream Superset RBAC would technically allow access. Evidence should show policy enforcement occurs before provider-owned REST execution.
accepted_payload.rationale: The service boundary is defined around governance taking precedence over raw backend reachability, with backend-resolved references and allowlist enforcement called out repeatedly.
accepted_payload.title: Verify governed reference resolution and allowlist enforcement before backend calls

accepted_payload.body: Verification should prove that optional controls such as `limit`, `asset_type`, `visualization_type`, `audience`, and `backend_options` behave exactly within contract bounds. Checks should confirm documented defaults are applied when omitted, closed-value fields reject unsupported values, and backend options permit only bounded provider controls such as selected fields, pagination, rendering format, preview format, or metadata-only options. Verification should explicitly reject arbitrary SQL execution, database switching, unbounded export, hidden publication, and arbitrary mutation payloads through backend options.
accepted_payload.rationale: The source spec and input contracts define bounded provider controls as a major governance boundary and specify default, clarify, or deny behavior for these inputs.
accepted_payload.title: Verify bounded backend options, defaults, and closed-value controls

accepted_payload.body: Verification should prove that discovery, bounded answers, previews, drafts, approval requests, denials, and clarification stops all emit durable, searchable, audit-friendly records. Checks should confirm records preserve enough context to explain what was requested, what governance boundary applied, and why the resulting posture was returned. Where implemented, verification should also assert presence of lineage identifiers such as task ID, invocation ID, client reference ID, and parent invocation ID so outcomes can be traced across requests.
accepted_payload.rationale: Audit durability, searchability, and lineage are explicit baseline requirements, but exact record schemas are not fully specified in the supplied evidence.
accepted_payload.title: Verify audit-friendly records and lineage across success, denial, approval, and clarification outcomes

accepted_payload.body: Verification should confirm that each of the six governed capabilities is treated as atomic at the ANIP contract layer and does not expose child capability composition, raw Superset REST operations, or MCP-style tool surfaces to callers. Checks should validate that any native Superset REST calls remain internal adapter behavior and that external verification focuses on governed inputs, governed outcomes, and policy enforcement rather than internal orchestration leakage.
accepted_payload.rationale: Completed developer evidence explicitly marks all six capabilities as atomic and states native Superset REST calls are provider-owned internals, not ANIP child steps.

capability-identity:canonical-ids.note: Confirmed. These six Superset capability IDs are the canonical governed analytics surface for this showcase.

actor_expectations.action_formalization: Can discover allowed analytics context and ask bounded analytics questions. May receive preview-style results only. Must not submit raw SQL, request unrestricted exports, bypass dataset allowlists, or switch to protected databases.
actor_expectations.approval_formalization: Does not approve governed changes. Read-only and preview interactions should not require this actor to obtain approval unless the request shifts into publish, save, or dataset-creation flows.
actor_expectations.summary_formalization: Business user who explores allowed analytics context and asks governed questions against allowlisted datasets and metrics.
actor_expectations.visibility_formalization: Can see only actor-visible and allowlisted datasets, charts, dashboards, metrics, and dimensions within approved workspace scope. Sensitive, owner-level, row-level, customer-level, or otherwise restricted dimensions may be hidden even if the backend could expose them.

actor_expectations.action_formalization: Can create chart previews, prepare dashboard drafts, and submit chart publication requests with a stated reason. Works in preview-first mode by default and cannot silently save or publish assets.
actor_expectations.approval_formalization: Must request approval for saving or updating charts and for any dashboard publish step implied by draft preparation. Cannot bypass approval gates.
actor_expectations.summary_formalization: User who turns governed analytics intent into chart previews, chart publish requests, and dashboard draft proposals.
actor_expectations.visibility_formalization: Can work only within allowlisted datasets, metrics, dimensions, chart previews, and dashboard scopes that are visible to the actor. Hidden or restricted dimensions and non-allowlisted assets remain unavailable.

actor_expectations.action_formalization: Can review publication requests and decide whether a governed chart save or update should proceed within approved scope.
actor_expectations.approval_formalization: Explicitly identified by the source as an approver for chart publication requests. Dashboard and dataset approval authority may overlap, but that is not stated clearly enough to assume.
actor_expectations.summary_formalization: Named approver role for governed analytics publication decisions.
actor_expectations.visibility_formalization: Needs visibility into the governed chart preview or dashboard scope under review, along with the stated publication reason and relevant governed context.

actor_expectations.action_formalization: Can review and approve chart save or update requests that are routed through governed publication flows.
actor_expectations.approval_formalization: Explicitly identified by the source as an alternative approver to analytics owner for chart publication requests. The brief does not confirm whether this actor also approves dashboard publication or dataset creation.
actor_expectations.summary_formalization: Administrative approver who can authorize governed chart publication when that authority is assigned to BI administration.
actor_expectations.visibility_formalization: Needs review visibility into the governed asset scope, publication reason, and any relevant allowlist or policy context for the request being approved.

actor_expectations.action_formalization: Can prepare dataset drafts by stating dataset purpose, query intent, and optional source tables or metrics. Cannot provide raw SQL, create unapproved saved queries, or bypass protected database restrictions.
actor_expectations.approval_formalization: Dataset draft creation is approval-gated, but the approving actor is not named in the source. This actor should be treated as a requester, not an approver.

product_alignment.approval_posture_formalization: Discovery, bounded question answering, and chart preview can proceed within governed limits, but saving or updating charts, publishing dashboards, and creating datasets are approval-gated and must stop for analytics owner or BI admin review before execution.
product_alignment.governed_behavior_formalization: The product only exposes governed analytics capabilities backed by native Superset REST APIs. It limits work to actor-visible and allowlisted datasets, metrics, dimensions, and scopes; defaults chart creation and similar exploratory work to preview-only or read-only behavior; asks for clarification when required context is missing; denies raw SQL, raw exports, protected database access, and publication bypass; and keeps outcomes audit-friendly.

required_behaviors: The service should deny disallowed operations consistently across actors unless a separate approved capability exists.
No hidden fallback to raw backend tools should occur.
Denial reasons should be explicit enough for user understanding and audit review.

service_topology_bindings.implementation_notes:
Accepted assistant guidance:
- Formalize the capability surface each service must expose: Define the stable capability contracts each service is expected to own, including which actions are read-only, which require approval, and which must stop for clarification.
  Why: The generator and verifier need stable capability ownership, not only narrative service descriptions.
- Describe the required coordination edges between services: Capture where one service depends on another for bounded context, approvals, or downstream execution so coordination can be represented explicitly instead of as hidden glue.
  Why: Coordination edges are easier to validate when they are declared before implementation.
- Keep approval boundaries outside the main happy path: Identify which service should prepare a proposed action and which surface or role must approve it before the mutation boundary is crossed.
  Why: Approval semantics should be explicit in service design rather than buried in one implementation path.
- Preserve explanation and traceability across service boundaries: Define which service boundaries must preserve explanation, lineage, or durable evidence so later verification can reconstruct why a result or stop condition occurred.
  Why: Cross-service traceability should be deliberate, not inferred after implementation exists.

actor_expectations.action_formalization: Can discover allowed analytics context and ask bounded analytics questions. May receive preview-style results only. Must not submit raw SQL, request unrestricted exports, bypass dataset allowlists, or switch to protected databases.
actor_expectations.approval_formalization: Does not approve governed changes. Read-only and preview interactions should not require this actor to obtain approval unless the request shifts into publish, save, or dataset-creation flows.
actor_expectations.summary_formalization: Business user who explores allowed analytics context and asks governed questions against allowlisted datasets and metrics.
actor_expectations.visibility_formalization: Can see only actor-visible and allowlisted datasets, charts, dashboards, metrics, and dimensions within approved workspace scope. Sensitive, owner-level, row-level, customer-level, or otherwise restricted dimensions may be hidden even if the backend could expose them.

actor_expectations.action_formalization: Can create chart previews, prepare dashboard drafts, and submit chart publication requests with a stated reason. Works in preview-first mode by default and cannot silently save or publish assets.
actor_expectations.approval_formalization: Must request approval for saving or updating charts and for any dashboard publish step implied by draft preparation. Cannot bypass approval gates.
actor_expectations.summary_formalization: User who turns governed analytics intent into chart previews, chart publish requests, and dashboard draft proposals.
actor_expectations.visibility_formalization: Can work only within allowlisted datasets, metrics, dimensions, chart previews, and dashboard scopes that are visible to the actor. Hidden or restricted dimensions and non-allowlisted assets remain unavailable.

actor_expectations.action_formalization: Can review publication requests and decide whether a governed chart save or update should proceed within approved scope.
actor_expectations.approval_formalization: Explicitly identified by the source as an approver for chart publication requests. Dashboard and dataset approval authority may overlap, but that is not stated clearly enough to assume.
actor_expectations.summary_formalization: Named approver role for governed analytics publication decisions.
actor_expectations.visibility_formalization: Needs visibility into the governed chart preview or dashboard scope under review, along with the stated publication reason and relevant governed context.

actor_expectations.action_formalization: Can review and approve chart save or update requests that are routed through governed publication flows.
actor_expectations.approval_formalization: Explicitly identified by the source as an alternative approver to analytics owner for chart publication requests. The brief does not confirm whether this actor also approves dashboard publication or dataset creation.
actor_expectations.summary_formalization: Administrative approver who can authorize governed chart publication when that authority is assigned to BI administration.
actor_expectations.visibility_formalization: Needs review visibility into the governed asset scope, publication reason, and any relevant allowlist or policy context for the request being approved.

actor_expectations.action_formalization: Can prepare dataset drafts by stating dataset purpose, query intent, and optional source tables or metrics. Cannot provide raw SQL, create unapproved saved queries, or bypass protected database restrictions.
actor_expectations.approval_formalization: Dataset draft creation is approval-gated, but the approving actor is not named in the source. This actor should be treated as a requester, not an approver.
actor_expectations.summary_formalization: User who prepares a virtual or semantic dataset draft through governed intent rather than raw SQL.
actor_expectations.visibility_formalization: Can work only against visible and allowlisted database and source-table scope exposed by the governed service. Protected databases and non-allowlisted sources remain unavailable.

product_alignment.approval_posture_formalization: Discovery, bounded question answering, and chart preview can proceed within governed limits, but saving or updating charts, publishing dashboards, and creating datasets are approval-gated and must stop for analytics owner or BI admin review before execution.
product_alignment.governed_behavior_formalization: The product only exposes governed analytics capabilities backed by native Superset REST APIs. It limits work to actor-visible and allowlisted datasets, metrics, dimensions, and scopes; defaults chart creation and similar exploratory work to preview-only or read-only behavior; asks for clarification when required context is missing; denies raw SQL, raw exports, protected database access, and publication bypass; and keeps outcomes audit-friendly.

required_behaviors: The service should deny disallowed operations consistently across actors unless a separate approved capability exists.
No hidden fallback to raw backend tools should occur.
Denial reasons should be explicit enough for user understanding and audit review.

service_topology_bindings.implementation_notes:
Accepted assistant guidance:
- Formalize the capability surface each service must expose: Define the stable capability contracts each service is expected to own, including which actions are read-only, which require approval, and which must stop for clarification.
  Why: The generator and verifier need stable capability ownership, not only narrative service descriptions.
- Describe the required coordination edges between services: Capture where one service depends on another for bounded context, approvals, or downstream execution so coordination can be represented explicitly instead of as hidden glue.
  Why: Coordination edges are easier to validate when they are declared before implementation.
- Keep approval boundaries outside the main happy path: Identify which service should prepare a proposed action and which surface or role must approve it before the mutation boundary is crossed.
  Why: Approval semantics should be explicit in service design rather than buried in one implementation path.
- Preserve explanation and traceability across service boundaries: Define which service boundaries must preserve explanation, lineage, or durable evidence so later verification can reconstruct why a result or stop condition occurred.
  Why: Cross-service traceability should be deliberate, not inferred after implementation exists.

business_constraints.assistant_drafted_requirements_summary:
Expose only governed analytics capabilities to agents: The product shall present a governed analytics capability surface for discovering allowed analytics context, answering bounded analytics questions, creating chart previews, requesting chart publication, preparing dashboard drafts, and preparing dataset drafts. Agents shall not receive raw SQL, raw chart, raw dashboard, raw database, or MCP-style tool exposure.
Use native Superset REST APIs as the backend execution binding: The product shall integrate with Apache Superset through native REST APIs as the durable backend for governed analytics operations. The implementation shall treat MCP only as a comparison surface and not as the execution interface for the governed contract.
Provide governed discovery of actor-visible analytics context: The product shall support discovery of allowed datasets, charts, dashboards, metrics, and dimensions within an actor-visible workspace scope. Discovery results shall be limited to allowlisted and visible assets and returned as read-only catalog summaries.
Answer analytics questions only within bounded dataset and metric constraints: The product shall answer analytics questions only when the request is anchored to an allowed dataset and governed metric or dimension context. Execution shall remain bounded by declared dataset scope, metric and dimension constraints, result grain, row limits, and audit constraints. Raw SQL shall not be accepted as an input.
Default creation workflows to preview-only outcomes: The product shall default chart creation and dashboard preparation workflows to preview-only behavior. Preview flows shall not save or publish artifacts unless an explicit approval-gated path is completed.
Require approval for publication and dataset creation actions: The product shall require explicit approval before saving or updating charts, publishing dashboards, or creating virtual or semantic dataset artifacts. The system shall capture a publish or creation reason and stop at an approval boundary until an authorized analytics owner or BI administrator approves the request.
Return clarification-required outcomes when required context is missing: The product shall return a clarification-required outcome when required context is missing, including missing dataset reference, metric, chart preview reference, dashboard scope, or publish reason. The clarification response shall identify the missing governed input needed to continue.
Enforce allowlists and sensitive-dimension restrictions beyond backend RBAC: The product shall enforce dataset, chart, dashboard, and database allowlists and may restrict owner-level, row-level, customer-level, or otherwise sensitive dimensions even when the downstream platform would technically allow access. Governance rules shall take precedence over raw backend reachability.
Limit backend options to bounded provider controls: The product shall allow backend options only for bounded provider controls such as selected fields, pagination, chart rendering format, or preview output format. Backend options shall not permit arbitrary SQL execution, unbounded export, database switching, allowlist bypass, or hidden publication behavior.
Deny prohibited raw access and bypass behaviors: The product shall deny requests for raw SQL, raw exports, unrestricted SQL execution, protected database access, dashboard publication bypass, and unapproved saved-query creation. Denial outcomes shall be explicit and audit-friendly.
Produce audit-friendly records for governed actions, denials, and approvals: The product shall produce audit-friendly outcomes for discovery, bounded answers, previews, approval requests, denials, and clarification stops. Records shall preserve enough context to explain what was requested, what governance boundary applied, and why the system returned the resulting posture.
Require real integration evidence before contract generation: Before contract generation, the product definition process shall include real integration evidence for the downstream Superset operations in scope, such as uploaded API documentation, schemas, permission matrices, live discovery, or manually entered operations. The process shall not infer backend operations from project name or domain alone.

shape.name: Superset governed fronting service shape
notes: Source explicitly declares Service ID `superset-governance-service` and describes a centralized ANIP fronting service.
Public service design should expose only governed analytics capabilities, not raw Superset APIs, MCP tools, SQL, or internal adapter operations.
Approval, clarification, and audit semantics are part of the service behavior and should be represented in contracts and workflow states.

services.name: Superset Governance Service
responsibilities: Expose the governed public analytics capability surface to agents.
Enforce actor-visible dataset, chart, dashboard, and database scope allowlists.
Apply metric and dimension governance, result-grain restrictions, row limits, and preview-only defaults.
Require clarification when required context such as dataset, metric, chart preview, dashboard scope, or publish reason is missing.
Gate chart publication, dashboard publication-related actions, and dataset draft creation behind approval semantics.
Deny raw SQL, raw exports, protected database access, allowlist bypass, hidden publication, and unapproved saved-query creation.
Map enterprise SSO identities to Superset actors and preserve audit-friendly outcomes.
Bind governed requests to native Superset REST APIs where deployment policy allows.
